Facts.text: Thutmose III, Pharaoh of Egypt, dies (according to the Low Chronology of the 18th Dynasty).
Goths lay siege to Rome
Trpimir II succeeds to the Croatian throne.
The Battle of Castagnaro begins.
Founder of the Safavid dynasty, Ismail I, is crowned Shah of Persia (rules till 1524)
Geuzen army leaves Walcheren to return to Oosterweel
Albert VII Archduke of Austria occupies Amiens, France
The Frondeurs (French rebels) and the French government sign the Peace of Rueil.
NY approves new code guaranteeing Protestants religious rights
Mt Etna in Sicily erupts in its largest recorded eruption, killing 15,000
1st English daily newspaper "Daily Courant" publishes
English auction house Sotheby's holds its first ever auction (of books) in London
US Army Corps of Engineers established (1st time)
Samuel Mulliken is 1st to obtain more than one US patent
Battle at Kurdla India: Mahratten beat Mogols
Citizenship granted to Prussian Jews
1st normal school in US opens, Concord Academy, Concord, Vermont
US War Department creates the Bureau of Indian Affairs
HMS Beagle anchors off Valparaiso, Chile
Louis-Hippolyte Lafontaine and Robert Baldwin become the first Prime Ministers of the Province of Canada to be democratically elected under a system of responsible government
Women's Medical College of Pennsylvania opens, 2nd female medical school in the US
Confederate convention in Montgomery adopts constitution (US Civil War)
Dale Dike on Humber River crumbles killing at least 240, in Sheffield, Yorkshire, England
The Great Sheffield Flood: the largest man-made disaster ever to befall England kills over 250 people in Sheffield
Giuseppe Verdi's opera "Don Carlos" premieres in Paris
The West first learns of the Giant Panda via French missionary Armand David who receives a skin from a hunter
Construction of the Seven Sisters Colliery, South Wales, begins; located on one of the richest coal sources in Britain
The Meiji Japanese government officially annexes the Ryukyu Kingdom into what would become the Okinawa prefecture
Intercollegiate Lacrosse Association organized in Princeton, New Jersey
Great blizzard of '88 strikes northeastern USA
1st public basketball game (Springfield, Massachusetts)
Boston opens its "palace for the people" Boston Public Library’s McKim Building in Copley Square designed by Charles Follen McKim - the first major Beaux Arts building in the US
Spanish cruiser Reina Regente sinks in Straits of Gibraltar, over 400 die
A meteorite enters the earth's atmosphere and explodes over New Martinsville, West Virginia. The debris causes damage but no human injuries are reported.
Cincinnati Enquirer reports Baltimore manager John McGraw signed Cherokee Indian Tokohoma, who is really black 2nd baseman Charlie Grant
Stanley Cup: Ottawa Silver Sevens beat Brandon Wheat City, 2-0 at the Aberdeen Pavilion, Ottawa for a 2 game sweep and back-to-back titles
Stanley Cup, Dey's Arena, Ottawa, ON: Ottawa Senators beat Rat Portage Thisles, 5-4 for 2-1 challenge series victory
First Stanley Cup challenge game to be played in three 20-minute periods (formerly 30-minute halves), Quebec beats Moncton, 9-3 on way to series sweep
The British declare a blockade of all German ports
1st NHL championship game ever played, Toronto Arenas beats Montreal Canadiens 7-3 in 1st of 2 game set (second game on March 13)
British forces occupy Baghdad, the capital of Mesopotamia, after Turkish forces evacuated
Conservationists John Merriam, Madison Grant, and Henry Fairfield Osborn found "Save the Redwoods League" - a non-profit forest conservation land trust in San Francisco, California founded
General strike in Germany crushed
Syria proclaims Emir Feisal king after the country has fought off French domination
Western Hockey Championship: Vancouver Millionaires (PCHA) sweep Regina Capitals, in 2 games
3rd term of Belgium Theunis government begins
Eden Phillpotts' "Farmer's Wife" premieres in London
Eamon da Valera ends leadership of Sinn Féin
1st armored commercial car hold-up in US (Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania)
1st boxing Golden Gloves tournament
William Taft, American 27th President & Chief Justice buried in Arlington Cemetery
Ready for Labour and Defence of the USSR, abbreviated as GTO, is introduced in the Soviet Union
Bank of Canada first opens on Wellington Street, Ottawa
Artur Seyss-Inquart replaces Kurt von Schuschnigg as Chancellor of Austria; German troops also entered the country
1st deportation train leaves Paris for Auschwitz Concentration Camp
Japanese troops land on North Sumatra
Nazi Militia forms in Netherlands
Dutch resistance fighter Joop Westerweel arrested
1,000 allied bombers harass Essen, 4,662 ton bombs
Flemish nazi collaborator Maria Huygens sentenced to death
Jewish Agency of Jerusalem bombed
WBAL TV channel 11 in Baltimore, MD (CBS) begins broadcasting
1st woman army doctor commissioned (FM Adams)
Charles Van Doren finally loses on US TV game show "Twenty-One" after winning $129,000 - later revealed to be fixed
American B-47 accidentally drops nuclear bomb 15,000 ft on a family home in Mars Bluff, South Carolina; creates crater 75 ft across, bomb without its nuclear capsule
Starting this season, AL batters are required to wear batting helmets
Pioneer 5 launched into solar orbit between Earth & Venus
Then NHL record 40 penalties, Black Hawks & Maple Leafs (20 each)
Somalia drops diplomatic relations with Great Britain
A fire at two ski resorts in Numata, Japan kills 31 people.
Anti-Zionist Clandestine Radio Voice of El Assifa starts transmitting
Iraqi Ba'th Party recognizes Kurd nation
OPEC threatens "appropriate sanctions" against companies that "fail to comply with ... any action taken by a Member Country in accordance with [OPEC] decisions."
Mount Etna in Sicily erupted
Rhino Records store in Claremont, California offers customers 5 cents to take home "Partridge Family" actor Danny Bonaduce's debut solo album
Rightist military coup in Portugal under General António de Spínola fails
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Hanafi Muslims hold 130 hostages in Washington, D.C.
Terrorists attack mail truck at Tel Aviv, 45 killed
USF-led Bill Cartwright scores 23 points as the Dons oust NC
Randy Hold receives 67 min in penalties in a 60 min NHL hockey game
Johnny Mize and Rube Foster are elected to the Baseball Hall of Fame
Detroit Pistons play the Chicago Bulls, with final score of Detroit 152 to Chicago 144 and Detroit having 20 blocked shots
Failed military coup under Rambocus and Hawker in Suriname
Harrison Williams (Sen-D-NJ) resigns rather than face expulsion
US boycotts Libyan crude oil
Ice Pairs Championship at Helsinki won by Valova & Vasiliev (Soviet Union)
1 million days since the foundation of Rome on April 21, 753 BC
Japanese probe Sakigake flies by Halley's Comet at 6.8 million km
NFL adopts instant replay rule
British pound note ceases to be legal tender, replaced by one pound coin
Utrecht conservatory destroyed by fire
Lithuania declares its Independence from the Soviet Union
John Smith, US amateur wrestler, wins James E Sullivan Award
Eduardo Frei succeeds Patricio Aylwin as President of Chile
-36.8°F (-38.2°C) in Chosedachar, Komi-district, on 67°N
President Nazarbajev disbands Kazakhstan parliament
Yolanda Chen hop-skip-jumps world indoor record 15.03m
John Winston Howard becomes the 25th Prime Minister of Australia. His term in office is the second longest in Australian history, ending December 3, 2007.
Mark Waugh scores 110 v NZ for his third century of the World Cup
The EU Database Directive is passed
San Francisco Giant J T Snow suffers a fractured eye socket when hit by a pitch
Infosys becomes the first Indian company listed on the NASDAQ stock exchange.
Nicholas Winton (93), former British stockbroker who helped 669 young Jews flee Czechoslovakia on the eve of the Nazi invasion in 1939, receives knighthood from Queen Elizabeth at Buckingham Palace
The International Criminal Court holds its inaugural session in The Hague.
Terrorists explode simultaneous bombs on Madrid's rail network ripping through a commuter train and rocking three stations, killing 190
Miguel Rodríguez Orejuela, co-founder of the Cali Cartel, is extradited to the United States
Winnenden school shooting - 17 people are killed at a school in Germany.

9.0 magnitude earthquake strikes 130 km (80 miles) east of Sendai, Japan, triggering a tsunami killing thousands of people and causing the second worst nuclear accident in history at Fukushima nuclear plant
US soldier kills 16 civilians in Afghanistan
European Union bans the sale of cosmetics that have been tested on animals
Falkland Islands’ sovereignty referendum: 99.8% choose to remain an Overseas Territory of the United Kingdom
North Korea cuts the phone line with South Korea, breaching the 1953 armistice
Refugees from Syria pour into the Kingdom of Jordan
At least 65 killed in landslide at rubbish dump near Addis Ababa, Ethopia
Greek football Super League suspended after PAOK Salonika's president Ivan Savvidis invades pitch with a gun after goal disallowed
11-year bull market ends as the Dow Jones Industrial Average falls more than 20%, becoming a bear market
NBA suspends 2019-20 season until further notice after Utah Jazz center Rudy Gobert tests positive for COVID-19
Smallest dinosaur ever discovered - skull preserved in piece of amber smaller than a fingertip from a mine in Myanmar, reported in "Nature"
Digital art work "Everydays: The First 5,000 Days" a digital college by Beeple, with a non-fungible token, sells for record $69.3 million in online auction by Christie's
China's government appoints Li Qiang, a close ally of PresidentXi Jinping, as the country's new Premier
Fertilizer spill in Red Oak, Iowa, kills an estimated 789,000 fish along a 60-mile stretch of river after valve was left open at NEW Cooperative
Romanian police detainAndrew Tateand his brother following the prosecutor of the Bucharest Court of Appeal's order for a one-day detention
132-year old shipwreck rediscovered in Lake Superior of steel streamer "The Great Western" which sank in 1892 with the loss of 27 lives
Astronomers announce the discovery of 128 new moons orbiting the planet Saturn, bring the planet's total to 274 moons
NASA's space telescope, the Spherex observatory, is launched by SpaceX from California to study how galaxies are formed by observing their cosmic glow
Pakistani Jaffa Express train hijacked by the Balochistan Liberation Army travelling from Quetta to Peshawar -  results in a 30-hour siege and the deaths of over 50 people
World Air Quality Report identifies Byrnihat, India, as the world's most polluted city, with Chad, Bangladesh, Pakistan, the Democratic Republic of Congo and India the most polluted countries
Giovanni de' Medici chosenPope Leo X
Queen Annewithholds Royal Assent from the Scottish Militia Bill, the last time a British monarch vetoes legislation
Benjamin BannekerandPierre Charles L'Enfantbegin to lay out Washington, D.C.
Johann Sebastian Bach's "St Matthew Passion" is revived byFelix Mendelssohn, aged 20, conducting in Berlin, Germany
Giuseppe Verdi's opera "Rigoletto" premieres in Venice
Bowery Boys gang leaderWilliam Pooleaka "Bill the Butcher" is buried in Brooklyn with 155 carriages and 6,000 mourners
Abraham LincolnremovesGeorge McClellanas General-in-Chief of the US Army
Confederate GeneralStonewall Jacksonbegins evacuation of Winchester, Virginia
GeneralWilliam T. Sherman's Union forces occupies Fayetteville, North Carolina
Norwegian scientist and explorerFridtjof Nansensets out on his first expedition on a sea voyage to study Arctic zoology
Future Tango iconCarlos Gardeland his mother, Berthe Gardès emigrate to Buenos Aires, Argentina
British Prime MinisterLord Salisburyrejects peace overtures from the Boer leaderPaul Kruger(on 5 March) as demanding too-favourable terms
Eleftherios Venizelos, leader of the Liberal Party, wins the Greek elections again.
Rupert Brooke's poems "The Dead" and "The Soldier" are published in The Times Literary Supplement
Samuel Rothafel opens the 5,920 seat Roxy Theater in New York City, with silent film "The Love of Sunya" starringGloria Swanson; largest movie theatre at the time of construction
Hans Lutheris appointed as president of the Reichsbank.
Hermann Goeringofficially creates the Luftwaffe (German Air Force)
Bronko Nagurskiregains the World Wrestling title from Ray Steele in Minnesota, after losing to him a year earlier
FDRsigns Lend-Lease Bill, allowing US to provide material support to Great Britain's war effort in return for future use of land for US military bases in England
General MacArthurleaves Corregidor (Bataan) for Australia
US Army charges SenatorJoseph McCarthyused undue pressure tactics
US Defense Secretary Robert MacNamara orders the adoption by the US military of the M16 assault rifle, originally designed as the AR-15 byEugene Stoner
Gene Roddenberrybrings together a 16-page pitch for the original Star Trek series
IndonesiaPresident Sukarnosigns the 'Supersemar' order, giving army commander Lt. Gen.Suhartoauthority to do whatever he "deemed necessary" to restore order
Military coup led by IndonesianGeneral Suhartobreaks out
Pink Floydreleases 1st single "Arnold Layne"
Dmitri Shostakovichcompletes his 12nd string quartet
Episode of "The Monkees" TV show features musicianFrank Zappaposing as Mike Nesmith and vice versa, debating qualities of each other's music
Otis Reddingis the first person in the US to posthumously receive gold record for his single "(Sittin' On) The Dock of the Bay"
12th Grammy Awards: Aquarius; Crosby Stills & Nash;Peggy Leewin
Jim Morrisonleaves for Paris to re-orient himself emotionally and creatively, and to avoid the jail sentence given to him in Miami, Florida. He never returns to the US.
Chile constitution takes effect,Augusto Pinochet's second term begins
Ice Dance Championship at Helsinki, Finland won byJayne TorvillandChristopher Dean(Great Britain)
Mikhail GorbachevreplacesKonstantin Chernenkoas Soviet leader
New York IslandersMike Bossy, 1st NHLer to score 50 goals in 9 straight seasons
Janet Jacksonsigns $40M 3 album deal with Virgin records
Monica SelesendsSteffi Graf's streak of 186 weeks ranked as #1
Sinn Féin party leaderGerry Adamsarrives in the US
BeatlePaul McCartneyis knighted byQueen Elizabeth II
Michelle Bacheletis inaugurated as the first female president of Chile
Plácido Domingonamed "The King of Singers" in BBC Music Magazine, based on voting by 16 renowned opera critics for the April 2008 issue
Sebastián Piñerabecomes Chilean president for the 1st time
Laureus World Sports Awards, Municipal Theater, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil: Sportsman:Usain Bolt; Sportswoman: Missy Franklin; Team: FC Bayern Munich
China's National People's Congress approves removal of term limits for a leader, allowingXi Jinpingto be president for life
Superhero movie "The Black Panther" becomes the fifthMarvelmovie to earn $1 billion worldwide
Algerian PresidentAbdelaziz Bouteflika(82) changes his mind saying he will not seek a fifth term and postpones elections after mass protests
Israeli President Reuven Rivlin, refutes Prime MinisterBenjamin Netanyahu's statement “Israel is the nation state of the Jewish people – and only it.”
Former Hollywood producerHarvey Weinsteinis sentenced to 23 years in prison for a criminal sex act and rape in New York
BritishPrince Williamsays the royal family is “very much not a racist family” in first public comments since interview by his brother Harry and wife Meghan
American rapperLil Durkreleases his 7th studio album "7220", debuts at #1 on the US Billboard 200 chart
Catherine, Princess of Wales, apologizes for "confusion" caused by releasing an edited family photo after news agencies issue a "kill" notice over digital manipulation
Former Philippines presidentRodrigo Duterteis arrested in Manila after the International Criminal Court issued a warrant accusing him of crimes against humanity over his "war on drugs"
Icon veneration officially re-instated in Hagia Sophia Cathedral in Constantinople

The Flagstaff War: In New Zealand, Chiefs Hone Heke and Kawiti lead 700 Māoris to chop down the British flagpole and drive settlers out of the British colonial settlement of Kororareka because of breaches of the 1840 Treaty of Waitangi.
COVID-19 declared a pandemic by the head of the World Health OrganizationTedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, after 121,564 cases worldwide and 4,373 deaths
Torquato Tasso, Italian Renaissance poet (Aminta, Apologia), born in Sorrento, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1595)
Henric Laurenszoon Spieghel, Dutch Renaissance writer and poet (Hertspiegel), born in Amsterdam (d. 1612)
Isaac Elsevier, Dutch book publisher (House of Elzevir), born in Leiden (d. 1651)
Heinrich Georg Neuss, German composer, Lutheran theologian, and poet, born in Elbingerode, Principality of Brunswick-Wolfenbüttel (d. 1716)
Giovanni Veneziano, Italian composer, born in Naples, Kingdom of Naples, Spanish Empire (d. 1742)
Henry Benedict Stuart, Jacobite pretender to the throne of Great Britain and Roman Catholic cardinal, born in Rome (d. 1807)
Otto Friedrich Müller, Danish Naturalist who was a pioneer in the study of microorganisms including bacteria, diatoms, and infusoria, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1784)
Robert Treat Paine, American judge and Founding Father (signed US Declaration of Independence), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1814)
Benjamin Tupper, American Continental Army officer, and pioneer to the Ohio Country, born in Sharon, Massachusetts (d. 1792)
Anthony Philip Heinrich, America's 1st "full-time" composer, born in Schönbüchel, Bohemia (d. 1861)
John McLean, American jurist (US Supreme Court Justice, 1829-61; Ohio Supreme Court Justice (1816-22) and US Postmaster General (1823-28), born in Morris County, New Jersey (d. 1861)
Ivan Nabokov, Russian general during the Napoleonic Wars, born in Russia (d. 1852)
Jan Frans Willems, Flemish writer and philologist, born in Boechout, Belgium (d. 1846)
Marsena Rudolph Patrick, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Hounsfield, New York (d. 1888)
Urbain Jean Joseph le Verrier, French astronomer (made calculations that proved existence of Neptune), born in Saint-Lô, France (d. 1877)
James Speed, American Attorney General (1864-66), born in Jefferson County, Kentucky (d. 1887)
William Vincent Wallace, Irish-American violinist (known as the 'Australian Paganini'), and composer (Maritana), born in Waterford, Ireland (d. 1865)
John Wilkins Whitfield, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Franklin, Tennessee (d. 1879)
Henry Tate, English sugar producer (established Tate Gallery in London), born near Chorley, England (d. 1899)
Marius Petipa, French ballet dancer and choreographer (Don Quiotte), born in Marseille, France (d. 1910)
William Ruffin Cox, American Confederate general and US Secretary of State (1893-1900), born in Scotland Neck, North Carolina (d. 1919)
Franz Melde, German physicist (Melde test), born in Großenlüder (d. 1901)
Ōkuma Shigenobu, Japanese politician (Prime Minister of Japan 1898, 1914-1916), born in Saga, Japan (d. 1922)
Edmund Kirby Jr, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Brownville, New York (d. 1863)
Antonio Cândido Gonçalves Crespo, Brazilian-born Portuguese poet, born in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil (d. 1886)
Thomas Hastings, American architect (NY Public Library), born in New York City (d. 1929)

Andrew Stoddart, English cricket batsman and captain (16 Tests, 2 x 100, HS 173; Middlesex CCC) and rugby union centre & captain (10 Tests; first captain Barbarians RFC), born in Westoe, England (d. 1915)
Wobbe de Vries, Dutch linguist, and writer (Something About Word Formation), born in Okswerd, Netherlands (d. 1942)
Arthur E Ochse, South African cricket batsman (2 Tests; Transvaal), born in Graaff-Reinet, South Africa (d. 1918)
Herm McFarland, American MLB outfielder (first grand slam in AL history), born in Des Moines, Iowa (d. 1935)
Louis Bachelier, French mathematician who pioneered mathematical finance introducing Brownian motion, born in Le Havre, France (d. 1946)
Carl Ruggles, American composer (Evocations), born in Marion, Massachusetts (d. 1971)
David Wijnkoop, Dutch socialist, and later communist politician, and journalist, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1941)
Alfred Léonard Loewenstein, Belgian financier and third-richest person in the world at the time, born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1928)[1][2][3]
Justus Hermann Wetzel, German composer, born in n Kyritz, Germany (d. 1973)
Niels Bjerrum, Danish chemist (ph tests), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1958)
Harry H. Laughlin, American eugenecist, born in Oskaloosa, Iowa (d. 1943)
Jan Lemaire, Dutch writer and actor (Village by the River, Beautiful Juliet), born in Amsterdam, Noord-Holland, Netherlands (d. 1982)
Lewi Pethrus, Swedish Pentecostal minister who founded Christian Democratic Party, born in Vargön, Sweden (d. 1974)
Vannevar Bush, American engineer and science administrator (developed 1st electronic analogue computer), born in Everett, Massachusetts (d. 1974)
Dick Tyldesley, English cricket spin bowler (7 Tests, 19 wickets, BB 3/50; Lancashire CCC), born in Westhoughton, England (d. 1943)
Henry Cowell, American pianist, composer (The Tides of Manaunaun; Aeolian Harp), and modern music promoter (New Musical Resources), born in Menlo Park, California (d. 1965)
(Irving Milford) "Miff" Mole, American jazz trombonist, and composer (Red Nichols and His Five Pennies - "Slippin' Around"; Sophie Tucker), born in Roosevelt, Long Island, New York (d. 1961)
Dorothy Gish, American film actress (Orphans of the Storm), born in Massillon, Ohio (d. 1968)
Frederick IX, King of Denmark (1947-72), born at Sorgenfri Palace, Denmark (d. 1972)
Josef Martin Bauer, German writer (including Nazi propaganda), radio dramatist, and novelist (As Far as My Feet Will Carry Me), born in Taufkirchen, Germany (d. 1970)
Dorothy Schiff, American owner and publisher (NY Post), born in New York City (d. 1989)
George Dickinson, New Zealand cricket fast bowler (3 Tests, 8 wickets; Otago, Wellington) and rugby union five-eighth (NZ 5 x tour matches; Otago RFU), born in Dunedin, New Zealand (d. 1978)
Lawrence Welk, American accordionist and orchestra leader (Lawrence Welk Show), born in Strasburg, North Dakota (d. 1992)
Ronald Syme, New Zealand classicist and historian, born in Eltham, New Zealand (d. 1989)
Cornelis Jan Bakker, Dutch nuclear physicist (Director of CERN), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1960)
Gilroy Roberts, American sculptor (Chief Engraver of the US Mint, 1948-64), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1992)
Hasan Ferit Alnar, Turkish composer, born in Istanbul, Ottoman Empire (d. 1978)
Zino Davidoff, Ukrainian--Swiss tobacco entrepreneur (cigars; desktop humidors), born in Novhorod-Siversky, Ukraine, Russian Empire (d. 1994)
Eleni Gatzoyiannis, Greek heroine (subject of "Eleni" by son Nicholas Gage about how she saved her children), born in Greece (d. 1948)
Helmuth James von Moltke, German jurist who opposed the Nazis (July 20th plot), born in Kreisau, Germany (d. 1945)
Jessie Matthews, British actress (Gangway, First a Girl), born in London, England (d. 1981)
Robert Havemann, German chemist and dissident, born in Munich, Germany (d. 1982)
Alan Gifford [John Lennox], American actor (Time Lock, 2001: A Space Odyssey), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1989)
Fitzroy Maclean, 1st Baronet, Scottish adventurer, soldier, writer and politician who was an inspiration for Ian Flemings James Bond, born in Cairo, Khedivate of Egypt, Ottoman Empire (d. 1996)
Robert Clifford Latham, English Pepys scholar, born in Audley, England (d. 1995)
Xavier Montsalvatge, Spanish composer (El gato con botas), born in Girona, Spain (d. 2002)
John Jacob Weinzweig, Canadian composer (The Enchanted Hill; Red Ear Of Corn), born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 2006)
Thomas Gray, English pioneering anaesthetist, born in Liverpool, England (d. 2008)
J. C. R. Licklider, American computer scientist and Internet pioneer, born in St. Louis Missouri (d. 1990)
Karl Krolow, German poet and translator, born in Hannover (d. 1999)
Vijay Hazare, Indian cricket batsman and captain (30 Tests, 7 x 100, HS 164no; Maharashtra, Central India, Baroda, Holkar), born in Sangli, India (d. 2004)
Ezra Jack Keats, American children's author known for "The Snowy Day" winner of the 1963 Caldecott Medal, born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1983)
Robert L. Carter, American civil rights activist and judge (Brown v. Board of Education, NAACP v. Alabama), born in Caryville, Florida (d. 2012)
Mercer Ellington, American composer, bandleader, and son of Duke Ellington, born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1996)
D. J. Enright, English poet and novelist (Some Men are Brothers), born in Royal Leamington Spa, England (d. 2002)
Astor Piazzolla, Argentinian  bandoneon player, and composer (Tango Nuevo), born in Mar del Plata, Argentina (d. 1992)
Frank Harary, American mathematician who pioneered modern graph theory, born in New York City (d. 2005)
Abdul Razak bin Hussain, Malaysian politician, Prime Minister of Malaysia (1970-77), born in Pekan, British Malaysia (d. 1976)
Cornelius Castoriadis, Greek philosopher and economist, born in Constantinople, Ottoman Empire (d. 1997)
José Luis López Vázquez, Spanish actor, director and comedian (La Cabina, Travels With My Aunt), born in Madrid, Spain (d. 2009)
Thom Kelling, Dutch singer, guitarist, and bandleader (Programa de Manha), born in 's-Gravenhage, Netherlands (d. 1968)
Ad den Besten, Dutch poet and publisher (Hymn Book for the Churches), born in Utrecht, Netherlands (d. 2015)[1]
Alexander Xaver Gwerder, Swiss writer, born in Thalwil, Zurich (d. 1952)
Terence Alexander, English actor (Bergerac), born in London, England (d. 2009)
Ilhan Mimaroglu, Turkish electronic music composer and musician, born in Istanbul, Turkey (d. 2012)
Malcolm Wells, American architect regarded as "the father of modern earth-sheltered architecture", writer, and solar energy consultant, born in Camden, New Jersey (d. 2009)[1]
Patricia Tindale, British architect and civil servant (school buildings, public housing), born in Chipping Barnet, Hertfordshire, England (d. 2011)
Thomas Starzl, American surgeon, performed 1st successful liver transplant, born in Le Mars, Iowa (d. 2017)
Alan Betts, British emeritus professor (Royal Veterinary College), born in Norfolk, England (d. 2005)
Raymond Jackson, British illustrator and political cartoonist (Evening Standard, 1952-97), born in Marylebone, London, England (d. 1997)
Robert Mosbacher, American businessman, yachtsman and political appointee (US Secretary of Commerce, 1989-92), born in Mount Vernon, New York (d. 2010)
Ron Todd, British trade unionist (TGWU General Secretary, 1985-92), born in Walthamstow, London, England (d. 2005)
Albert Salmi, American stage and screen character actor (Caddyshack; The Brothers Karamazov; Bus Stop), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1990)
Peter R. Hunt, British film director (On Her Majesty's Secret Service), born in London (d. 2002)
Erskine Childers, Irish writer and United Nations civil servant official, born in Dublin, Ireland (d. 1996)
Jackie McGlew, South African cricket batsman (34 Tests, 7 x 100, HS 255no; Natal), born in Pietermaritzburg, South Africa (d. 1998)
Józef Zapędzki, Polish sport shooter (Olympic gold 25m rapid fire pistol 1968, 72), born in Rokitno, Poland (d. 2022)
Timothy Carey, American actor (Paths of Glory, The Killing), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1994)
Bobby Winkles, American College Baseball Hall of Fame coach (Arizona State University) and manager (California Angels, Oakland A's), born in Tuckerman, Arkansas (d. 2020)
David Gentleman, British visual artist, illustrator, and muralist (Charing Cross underground station, born in London
Colin Jillings, New Zealand thoroughbred horse trainer (W. S. Cox Plate 1993 The Phantom Chance; 4 x Auckland Cup winners; 5 x NZ Derby winners), born in Auckland, New Zealand (d. 2022)
Peter Walters, British businessman (British Petroleum, 1954-90; CEO of Midland Bank, 1991-94; Chairman of SmithKline Beecham, 1994-2000), born in Birmingham, England (d. 2023)
Leroy Jenkins, American jazz violinist, avant-garde jazz and contemporary classical composer, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2007)
Nigel Lawson, British journalist and politician (UK Chancellor 1983-89 reformed tax laws), born in London, England (d. 2023)[1]
Valerie French, British actress (Jubal), born in London, England (d. 1990)
Rudy Hayden, Canadian country music and rockabilly singer ("Want Me A Woman"), and stage magician, born in Ethelbert, Manitoba
Saïd Amara, Algerian soccer midfielder (5 caps; Strasbourg, Béziers, Bordeaux) and manager (Algeria), born in Saïda, Algeria (d. 2020)
Sandra Milo [Salvatrice Greco], Italian actress (8½; Juliet of the Spirits), and television personality, born in Tunis, French Tunisia (d. 2024)
Walter Brueggeman, American theologian and advocate for the poor (The Prophetic Imagination), born in Tilden, Nebraska (d. 2025)
George Stamatoyannopoulos, Greek genetics researcher (founder of American Society of Gene and Cell Therapy), and educator, born in Athens, Greece (d. 2018)
Joep Straesser, Dutch composer (Blossom songs, Ramasasiri), born in Amsterdam, the Netherlands (d. 2004)
Sam Donaldson, ABC White House correspondent (Prime Time), born in El Paso, Texas
Sydney Burke, South African cricket fast bowler (2 Tests; 11-196 on Test debut v NZ 1961), born in Pretoria, South Africa (d. 2017)
Antonin Scalia, 105th Supreme Court Justice (1986-2016), born in Trenton, New Jersey (d. 2016)
Nathan Silver, American architect, writer (Lost New York), and educator (Columbia; Cambridge), born in Manhattan, New York City (d. 2025)[1]
Alexandra Zabelina, Russian fencer (Olympic gold USSR women's team foil 1960, 68, 72), born in Moscow, Russia (d. 2022)
John Ward, New Zealand cricket wicketkeeper (8 Tests; 17 dismissals; Canterbury), born in Timaru, New Zealand (d. 2021)
Milan Vukcevich, Yugoslavian chemist and a grandmaster of chess problem composition, born in Belgrade, Serbia (d. 2003)
(Leonardo) "Flaco" Jiménez, American Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award-winning Tex-Mex and conjunto accordionist (Texas Tornados), born in San Antonio, Texas (d. 2025)[1][2]
Lorraine Hunt, American businesswoman, and former Lieutenant Governor of Nevada, born in Niagara Falls, New York
